data.title: Golden Web3.0 Daily Report | US Says Agreement with Iran Expected
data.description:
DeFi data

1. Total market value of DeFi tokens: US$54.778 billion

DeFi Total Market Cap Data Source: coingecko

2. Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours: $51.79

Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours. Data source: coingecko

3. Assets locked in DeFi: $974.84 billion$100 million

Top 10 DeFi Projects by Locked Assets and Total Value Locked. Data Source: defillama

NFT Data

1. Total NFT Market Cap: $7.994 billion

2.482 billion NFT transaction volume in 24 hoursUSD

Headline

US Says Agreement with Iran Expected

On April 16, according to US sources on the 15th, US President Trump stated that the US is "very likely" to reach an agreement with Iran before King Charles III's visit to the US in late April. In an interview, Trump stated that the possibility of reaching an agreement is currently "very high." King Charles III and Queen Camilla are scheduled to visit the US from April 27 to 30.

AI Hot Topics 1. OpenAI: Accelerating the Construction of a Network Defense Ecosystem On April 16, OpenAI announced that it is accelerating the construction of a network defense ecosystem to protect everyone. 2. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang: Anthropic and OpenAI are about to go public, Nvidia may not invest further. On April 16, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang stated on the Dwarkesh podcast that in the field of large language models, Nvidia pledged to invest up to $10 billion in Anthropic last November and announced a $30 billion investment in OpenAI this February. Huang stated at a meeting in March that these may be Nvidia's last round of investment due to the upcoming IPOs of both companies.

3. Large Language Models May "Smuggle" Their Own Preferences in Distillation

A study published in *Nature* on April 15th shows that Large Language Models (LLMs) may "smuggle" their own preferences to other algorithms, even after removing the original features from the training data. These unwanted features may still persist. In one case, a model appeared to pass its preference for owls to other models through implicit signals in the data. This finding suggests that more thorough safety checks are needed when developing LLMs.

4. OpenAI Lobbies to Expand the Role of Artificial Intelligence in Life Sciences

April 16th, according to AXIOS: OpenAI is lobbying to expand the role of artificial intelligence in life sciences.

DeFi Hot Topics

1. Bless Project Team Transfers 300 Million BLESS Tokens from Two Solana Chain Addresses in the Past 9 Hours

On April 16th, the Bless project team transferred another 50 million BLESS tokens (worth $590,000) from the Solana chain to the BSC chain and continued on-chain selling. Currently, 56.76 million BLESS tokens have been sold on BSC, yielding 228,000 USDT and 416.4 BNB (worth $258,000). This morning, the project team transferred a total of 300 million BLESS tokens, with 200 million transferred to Bitget and the remaining 100 million being sold on the BSC chain.

4. US Spot Ethereum ETF Sees Net Inflow of $67.86 Million Yesterday

According to Trader T monitoring, on April 16th, the US Spot Ethereum ETF saw a net inflow of $67.86 million yesterday.

5. On-chain lending platform Votre completes $3.75 million seed round funding, led by a16z CSX

On April 16th, New York-based on-chain lending platform Votre announced the completion of a $3.75 million seed round funding, led by a16z's crypto startup accelerator a16z CSX, with participation from MaC Venture Capital and a group of angel investors. The company currently operates a non-custodial cryptocurrency lending platform based on the Base layer 2 network, helping cryptocurrency holders obtain liquidity without selling their assets. The new funds will be used to expand Votre's technical infrastructure and enhance the functionality of its lending platform.

Disclaimer: As a blockchain information platform, Jinse Finance publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish correct investment concepts and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Venture Capital: From Doriotte to Power Law
data.description:
Author: insights4vc Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

The history of venture capital is essentially a history of the institutionalization of risk. This story did not begin in the modern Silicon-based offices of Sandhill Road, but rather in the industrial heartland of pre-war France and the solemn, disciplined classrooms of Harvard Business School. This industry evolved from Georges Doriot's paternalistic, institutionally-driven philosophy to the radical, data-driven investment paradigm of modern Silicon Valley's power law model—a transformation far more than just a change in financial strategy.

It reveals the profound evolution of human society in identifying talent, allocating capital for the unknown, and ultimately building the future. Using Spencer E. Ant's *Creative Capital* and Sebastian Malabi's *Power Law* as our core perspectives, we trace this development trajectory, revealing the shifting power dynamics between creative holders and the capital providers who realize those creative ideas.

The Cognitive Logic of Risk: The underlying logic of venture capital differs fundamentally from traditional finance. Most financial sectors, whether foreign exchange markets, bond trading, or public stock exchanges, operate within a safe framework of normal distribution. In these systems, observations revolve around the mean, and extreme tail events are statistically rare enough to be managed as outliers. Bond traders seek stable returns, and hedge fund managers consider doubling their portfolios within three years a remarkable achievement. Venture capital, however, is a "grand slam business," not a typical home run business. In this world, outliers not only affect the mean, but they are the mean themselves. Marabi points out that the power law is a universal principle: winners widen the gap at an accelerating, exponential rate. In a typical venture capital fund, a very small number of investments—perhaps only 5% of the allocated capital—contribute more than 60% of the total return. This mathematical reality dictates a unique cognitive system: most ideas are insignificant; only those seemingly impossible ideas possess true value. This demands a specific type of investor: one who can accept a 90% failure rate, as long as the remaining 10% can fundamentally change the human condition. The evolution of venture capital is a shift from a qualitative, mentorship-based model to a quantitative, mathematically-driven approach that embraces the unconventional. As Vinod Khosla famously said, progress depends on "those who don't play by the rules." This idea was fully embodied in 2010 when Stanford geneticist Patrick Brown presented Khosla with an "absurd" presentation proposing to completely replace the meat processing industry with plant-based heme. Khosla's initial response was "That's impossible!", followed by a $3 million check. This encapsulates the modern venture capitalist mindset: if an idea has a one percent chance of success and corresponds to a trillion-dollar market, then this risk is not only acceptable but must be taken.

Industrial Roots and the Birth of ARD

To understand the modern era of "sovereign founders," we must first trace back to the paternalistic origins of the industry, represented by Georges Doriot. In *Creative Capital*, Ant meticulously recounts that Doriot came from the French industrial elite. His father, Auguste Doriot, was a pioneer of Peugeot automobiles, involved in the manufacture of some of the earliest cars, and won the 1894 Paris-Rouen endurance race, a rigorous test of mechanical reliability.

Arthur Rock and the Fairchild Rebellion

While Doriot was building his empire in Boston, a more radical form of finance was emerging in the West. The key turning point, which Malabi calls “the liberation of capital,” occurred in the summer of 1957—the same year DEC invested—at the Shockley Semiconductor Laboratory in Mountain View, California. Nobel laureate and father of semiconductors, William Shockley, was also a “tyrannical despot,” subjecting employees to lie detector tests and belittling their intelligence.

Eight top PhD researchers under Shockley, including Gordon Moore and Robert Noyce, decided to resign en masse. In the era of "organizational men" in the 1950s, such a collective defection was unheard of, and loyalty was the highest virtue in a company. The "Traitorous Eight" did not initially want to start their own business, but simply hoped to be employed by a better-managed company as a team. Their letter eventually reached Arthur Locke, a young MBA at Haydenstone investment bank in New York.

... Locke realized that top-tier research talent, coupled with the rebellious spirit displayed by this act, presented an unprecedented business opportunity. He persuaded them to abandon employers and start their own businesses. He raised $1.4 million from Sherman Fairchild to found Fairchild Semiconductor. This was the birth of "adventure capital": liberating talent from traditional hierarchies and creating the organizational structure best suited for innovation. Fairchild became the core engine of the Silicon Valley miracle: by 2014, 70% of Silicon Valley's publicly traded companies could be traced back to this act of rebellion. In 1961, Locke partnered with Tommy Davis to establish Davis-Locke, becoming the first venture capital firm to adopt a private limited partnership structure, further refining this model. This structural shift tolled the death knell for the Dorriote publicly traded company model. The Triumph of the Limited Partnership: ARD's failure to become a mainstream venture capital template demonstrates that the history of venture capital is shaped not only by investor psychology but also by legal and financial structures. As a publicly traded investment firm, ARD was subject to the strict constraints of the Investment Company Act and close oversight by the SEC. This structure made experimentation cumbersome, compensation inflexible, and incentives weak. In an industry where a very few outliers can contribute the majority of returns, the inability to share substantial profits with investment professionals is not a minor design flaw but a fatal one. Private limited partnerships offer a superior structure. The new venture capital firms are no longer publicly traded companies with dispersed shareholdings, but rather private contractual arrangements between limited partners who provide capital and general partners who find projects, manage post-investment activities, and share profits. This simultaneously solves several problems: reducing regulatory hurdles, strengthening incentive consistency, and allowing venture capitalists to be rewarded through incidental income rather than fixed salaries. In fact, the limited partnership structure provides a more suitable structure for power-law businesses, ultimately aligning asset class returns with managerial compensation. However, the victory of the limited partnership structure was not purely a private sector achievement. A series of public policy decisions gradually transformed venture capital from a niche financing method into a scalable institutional asset class. The first important signal came from the Small Business Investment Act of 1958. The Small Business Administration (SBIC) pointed out that this act stemmed directly from a 1957 Federal Reserve study: small businesses lacked credit support and struggled to keep up with technological changes. To address this, the federal government launched the SBIC program, with the SBIC licensing, regulating, and funding privately managed investment firms to provide long-term debt and equity financing to high-risk small businesses. In other words, the federal government did not invent venture capital, but it explicitly acknowledged the structural financing gap and began building an institutional framework to address it. Subsequent tax policies improved the economics of entrepreneurship and investment risk. In the late 1970s and early 1980s, the top federal tax rate on long-term capital gains was significantly reduced: Treasury data shows that the rate was 49% before the 1978 reforms, dropped to 28% after the 1978 Revenue Act, and further decreased to 20% after the 1981 Economic Recovery Tax Act. These changes did not alone create the modern venture capital industry, but they significantly increased the after-tax attractiveness of high-growth equity investments. In areas where returns are highly concentrated in a few blockbuster exits, lower capital gains taxes reinforced the incentive to fund high-risk innovation rather than investing in safer income-generating assets. The truly decisive turning point came from pension capital. As Paul Gampers and Josh Lerner noted, between 1946 and 1977, the inflow of new venture capital funds into the United States never exceeded several hundred million dollars annually, typically falling far short. A key change occurred in 1979 when the Department of Labor reinterpreted the ERISA “prudent person” rule. Prior to this, many pension trustees avoided venture capital because investing in a single startup could be deemed imprudent. The 1979 clarification allowed prudence to be assessed from the perspective of the entire portfolio and its diversification, rather than a single risk position. This rule change opened the door for pension funds to become principal limited partners in venture capital partnerships. Its impact was immediate and transformative. Venture capital fundraising rose from approximately $450 million in 1979 to over $5 billion in 1983. This was not merely growth, but a paradigm shift. Once pension funds entered the asset class, venture capital was no longer a semi-amateur activity funded by wealthy families, industrial patrons, and scattered institutions. It has become a replicable financial industry, with sufficient scale to support specialized funds, larger rounds, longer holding periods, and more ambitious technology bets. The modern venture capital ecosystem—focused funds, formal limited partnerships—general partnerships, and institutionalized search for outliers—relies on both the talent of individual investors and this regulatory deregulation. If the Doriot era gave venture capital moral legitimacy, the limited partnership era gave it financial scalability. The core lesson is that venture capital's rise to dominance is not solely due to smarter investors, but rather because organizational forms, tax treatments, and public policies ultimately align with the economics of high-risk innovation. The government did not replace private judgment, but it decisively shaped the conditions under which private judgment could be exercised on a large scale. Therefore, the rise of venture capital should not be understood merely as a market story, but rather as a story of institutional design. Culture and Social Networks The geographical shift of venture capital focus from Boston to Silicon Valley in the 1980s stemmed from what Annalee Saxonnin called "regional advantage." Boston's Route 128 was already a high-tech hub before Silicon Valley was named, and in 1965, the number of tech professionals there was three times that of Silicon Valley. However, the two places had diametrically opposed cultures. Boston companies were led by Harvard-background leaders, emulating General Motors, emphasizing self-sufficiency and hierarchical systems. In contrast, Silicon Valley developed a decentralized industrial system, with companies outsourcing parts and freely exchanging information in places like Walker Wheel Bar. This "networked" culture was further reinforced by California law's non-enforcement of non-compete agreements. Venture capitalists were the main disseminators of this network. Professionals like Bill Young of Sutter Hill Ventures would invite the “smartest people” to lunch, asking them to recommend the next “best person,” systematically building a network of relationships to quickly activate when new opportunities arose. In the 1970s, the “active interventionist capital” model replaced Doriot’s “paternalistic” model. Represented by Don Valentine of Sequoia Capital and Tom Perkins of Kleiner Perkins, venture capitalists of this era were successful managers at companies like Fairchild and Hewlett-Packard. They didn't just provide funding; they imprinted their will on the post-investment companies. Tom Perkins is known for his "risk-stage" financing. At Tientronics, he brought in advisors to mitigate technological risks before making additional investments; at Genentech, the first major biotechnology company, he urged the founders to outsource research to existing labs to conserve cash—this deep intervention yielded a 200-fold return for Kleiner Perkins. Don Valentine was equally assertive with Atari, forcing the company to focus on the "home ping" market and subsequently pushing for its sale to Warner Communications. This is a "finance beyond finance" model: investors skip spreadsheets and focus on assessing character, resilience, and the "book value of knowledge." Arthur Locke would ignore financial forecasts, go straight to the founder's resume, ask open-ended questions, and observe in long silences whether the entrepreneur would break down under pressure. If the founder "lives in an ideal world rather than in reality," Locke would abandon the project. Youth Rebellion and Sovereign Founders The most recent evolution of venture capital began at the turn of the century, with power shifting significantly towards entrepreneurs. This "youth rebellion" was ignited by the success of Google and Facebook, companies that not only achieved scale but also complete market dominance. In earlier times, venture capitalists like Don Valentine had the right to fire founders; in the Cisco case, the founder was ultimately ousted due to a conflict with the professional CEO hired by Valentine. However, by 2005, abundant capital and the emergence of brand growth equity changed the game. After being "hurt" by Sequoia Capital in the Plaxo project, Sean Parker advised Mark Zuckerberg on how to maintain control of Facebook. Peter Thiel's Founders Fund elevated this shift to an ideology. Thiel believed that venture capital should no longer "polish" founders through mentorship, but should embrace "corporate monarchy," seeking out "rough gems" and allowing them to lead unchallenged. This era introduced dual-class share structures: founders retained super voting rights after the IPO. SoftBank's Masayoshi Son and DST Global's Yuri Milner's "kingmaker" capital accelerated this shift. In 2009, Milner invested $200 million in Facebook, valuing the company at $10 billion, without demanding a board seat—a deal Zuckerberg couldn't refuse. This "non-intervention" model indicates that for the most promising outliers, venture capitalists are no longer parents or bosses, but service providers. The shift towards a founder-centric, risk-driven model has created both the greatest successes and the most infamous failures of the digital age. The "blitzscaling" model—mass hiring without rigorous vetting and launching unfinished products to quickly seize market share—thrives in a deliberately lax, "founder-friendly" ecosystem. Malabi emphasizes that the obsession with "lone geniuses" fostered arrogance at WeWork and Uber. The radical culture of Uber founder Travis Kalanick ultimately evolved into a "Shakespearean" tragedy of ambition and self-absorption; Benchmark Capital's Bill Gurley was forced to launch a campaign to remove the founder he had personally supported. Similarly, WeWork's expansion, backed by SoftBank, demonstrates that massive amounts of venture capital can sustain unprofitable business models for too long, ultimately destroying economic value. These failures point to "board contagion": a lack of board oversight leads to damage to corporate reputations due to leaders' unbridled impulses. The Ethics and Geopolitics of the New Future As venture capital becomes a global force, its social impact faces increasing scrutiny. The industry remains a tightly knit circle with severely insufficient diversity: as of 2020, women comprised only 16% of investment partners, and Black partners only 3%. This demographic "social clustering" leads to a disproportionate flow of funds to founders with backgrounds similar to investors, systematically excluding a large pool of talent. However, the positive value of venture capital remains strong. Although critics argue that VCs only pursue success, academic research confirms that VC-backed companies contribute 89% of listed companies' R&D spending and have a much higher success rate for IPOs than non-VC-backed companies. Between 1995 and 2019, VC-backed companies accounted for 47% of non-financial IPOs in the United States. Furthermore, this model has become a pillar of national power. China's rise as a technological competitor stems directly from the introduction of the Silicon Valley VC model. Partnering institutions like Sequoia China applied power-law logic to the Chinese internet, creating a digital economy comparable to that of the United States. In a world shifting from tangible assets (factories and machines) to intangible assets (software and data), deeply involved VC models are increasingly becoming the only effective way to allocate capital. Conclusion The evolution of venture capital from George Doriot's "Inception" to the modern power-law era reveals a fundamental shift in the way humans innovate. Doriot imbued the industry with ethical and institutional legitimacy, proposing that funding "noble" ideas was a worthwhile endeavor in mature societies. Arthur Locke provided "liberated capital," freeing talent from the rigid hierarchies of the 1950s. Valentine and Perkins refined tools for active intervention, eliminating "hot risk" and building industry pillars. Today, the industry pushes outlier logic to mathematical extremes. Despite the significant governance challenges brought about by the shift towards founder-led monarchy and blitzkrieg expansion, it remains the most efficient crucible for technological discovery to date. As a "third institution" between the market and the firm, it combines the strategic vision of the firm with the ruthless price signaling discipline of the market. The evolution of venture capital demonstrates that the future cannot be predicted by analyzing past patterns; it must be discovered through high-risk experiments by those who "play by their own rules." By supporting the impossible, this special group of financial professionals has made the modern world possible. Just as Auguste Doriot's father once asked his son, who came in second, "Why not first?", venture capital has institutionalized this question within the global economy, refusing to accept anything less than a radical transformation of the human condition.

data.title: A Comprehensive Look at the Real Threat of Quantum Computers to Bitcoin (Part 2)
data.description:
Written by: Eli Nagar   Translated by: Block unicorn

After publishing Part 1, I wanted to see if people were interested and if it was worth exploring further. Reader feedback made this decision very easy.

So, Part 2 is here.

Part 5: Google's Big News (March 2026)

Note: This section contains more technical information.

If you find this difficult to understand, here's a brief summary: A significant research paper published by Google, Stanford University, and other institutions shows that breaking Bitcoin's encryption is 20 times easier than previously thought, and requires fewer than 500,000 quantum computing units, not millions. Future quantum computers could crack a Bitcoin private key in about 9 minutes, less than the approximately 10 minutes required for Bitcoin transaction confirmation. This means attackers could potentially steal a Bitcoin transaction before it's completed, or even during the transaction itself. Bitcoin's latest upgrade, Taproot (2021), actually makes things worse from a quantum perspective. It exposes crucial information, giving quantum attackers an unlimited amount of time to crack it. Approximately 6.9 million BTC (worth hundreds of billions of dollars) are stored in address types vulnerable to this type of attack. Google used a clever technique (zero-knowledge proof) to prove their calculations were correct, validating the results without revealing the actual attack blueprint. There isn't a quantum computer powerful enough for that yet. But they believe they're 20 times closer to the goal, and the Bitcoin community needs to start preparing now. Still not enough? Let's continue. While I was researching this topic, Google released a major announcement. On March 30, 2026, Google's quantum AI team, in collaboration with Stanford University (Dan Boneh), the Ethereum Foundation (Justin Drake), and the University of California, Berkeley, published a 57-page paper titled "Protecting Elliptic Curve Cryptocurrencies from Quantum Vulnerabilities: Resource Estimation and Mitigation Measures." I read the entire paper. This paper fundamentally changes my understanding of this threat (compared, of course, to previous assumptions). Why this paper is important: Previous estimates suggested that breaking Bitcoin's encryption would require millions of physical qubits. Google's paper demonstrates that it can be done with fewer than 500,000 qubits, a 20-fold reduction. This doesn't mean Bitcoin faces an imminent danger, but it does mean the danger is closer than the community previously assumed.

Resources Reduced by 20x

The paper proposes two optimized quantum circuit designs for running Shor's algorithm to solve the 256-bit Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP-256), a mathematical problem used to protect Bitcoin signatures:

TOFFOLI Gate

A basic quantum logic gate that operates on three qubits. It is a universal building block for quantum computing. The number of Tooffoli gates in the circuit is a key indicator of computational cost; fewer gates result in faster execution and less error accumulation.

For ease of understanding, here is a comparison with previous estimates: This reduction comes from algorithmic optimization, not hardware advancements. Researchers applied two key techniques: state reuse (deriving multiple private keys from the public key in a single execution by reusing the initial phase estimate state) and division batching (running multiple instances of the algorithm in parallel and merging modulo division operations; this technique is known as the "Montgomery trick"). These improvements are purely mathematical refinements to the implementation of Shor's algorithm.

9-Minute Attack

This discovery shocked me. On superconducting quantum architectures (such as "fast clock" machines like Google's Willow chip series), such an attack can be completed in approximately 9 minutes.

... The paper provides precise calculations: assuming the control system's reaction time is 10 microseconds (the standard for superconducting qubits) and each Toffoli gate has a 50% overhead, then on a superconducting CRQC, 70 million or 90 million Toffoli gates can be resolved in 18 or 23 minutes. However, the first half of the algorithm (universal to all addresses) can be pre-computed. Starting from this "pre-processed state," the attack takes approximately 9 or 12 minutes, depending on the circuit variant.

Dynamic Attack

In this type of attack, the quantum attacker waits for the victim to broadcast a transaction (thus exposing the public key), then races against time to derive the private key and submits a competing fraudulent transaction before the original transaction is confirmed. The 9-minute attack makes this attack effective against Bitcoin's approximately 10-minute block time.

Static Attack

This type of attack targets cryptocurrencies whose public keys are already publicly available on the blockchain (such as P2PK, P2TR, and reused addresses). The attacker has unlimited time and does not need to race against time. The attacker can steal these cryptocurrencies at any time.

Taproot: Worse Than We Thought

As a Bitcoin enthusiast, I was deeply saddened to read this article. One of the most important findings relates to Taproot (P2TR), Bitcoin's most recent major upgrade (2021). The paper explicitly states that P2TR has the same vulnerability level as the original P2PK format from 2009. The reason is that P2TR stores the modified public key directly in the locking script without using a hash value for hiding. This means that the public key is exposed on the blockchain from the moment the Bitcoin is received, allowing quantum attackers to attack ECDLP offline indefinitely. The paper cites BIP-360's P2MR (Pay-to-Merkle-Root) proposal as a potential solution, essentially removing the quantum-vulnerable key path expenditure from P2TR. P2MR retains many advantages of Taproot while avoiding the reintroduction of static attack vulnerabilities. The paper also calculates, using data from the bigquery-public-data.crypto_bitcoin database, that approximately 6.9 million Bitcoins across all protocol types are vulnerable. This figure is significantly higher than the previously cited 4 to 5 million bitcoins, primarily because Taproot's output is now also included in the vulnerability's scope. The paper identifies four different ways public keys can be exposed: Weak address vulnerabilities, where locking scripts directly leak the public key (P2PK, P2TR, P2MS). Address reuse vulnerabilities, where unlocking scripts in past spending transactions leak the public key. Public memory pool exposure, where the public key is visible during the transaction confirmation process. Off-chain exposure means public keys are shared elsewhere (exchanges, blockchain explorers, off-chain records). The paper makes a key distinction between two types of quantum computers, which have drastically different implications for when and how attacks become feasible: Scenario 2 may come first. The paper points out that slow-clock architectures may reach the required number of qubits earlier than fast-clock architectures because they may face fewer engineering challenges in scaling. If this is the case, static attacks will be easier to occur than dynamic attacks, thus providing the community with a brief window during which only the leaked keys are at risk. However, the paper warns that "the time interval between the breaking of a 32-bit ECDLP and the breaking of a 256-bit ECDLP could be very short," meaning that once a cracking begins, progress could be extremely rapid. The most thought-provoking part of the paper, which I found most compelling, is Section 8, which explores a highly politically sensitive issue: what happens to dormant assets—Bitcoins held by lost keys, deceased owners, or abandoned wallets? The paper estimates that approximately 2.3 million Bitcoins are stored in dormant P2PK addresses (unused for at least 5 years), which could be "harvested" by quantum computers. They use their simulations to show that a high-speed CRQC can accumulate approximately 2,000 Bitcoins from a single, large wallet and process hundreds of thousands of addresses within days to months. The paper outlines three community solutions under discussion: The paper also proposes a fourth solution: a "bad debt sidechain," a dedicated sidechain for recovery (similar to a "bad bank" in traditional finance). CRQC operators would send recovered dormant assets to this sidechain. This sidechain would implement relevant processes, identifying the true owner and returning assets through off-chain proof-of-ownership, while controlling the speed of asset redeployment to prevent market chaos. The paper also proposes a legal framework that draws on existing abandoned property laws (such as marine salvage and the revised U.S. Uniform Unclaimed Property Act). Under this framework, dormant tokens recovered through quantum recovery will be treated as "salvaged" property, and legal procedures will be used to identify the rightful owner and manage the proceeds, similar to how liquidators handle bankrupt assets.

Part Six: Proposed Solutions

Okay, so the threat is real. The next question I want to answer is: What measures are we taking to address it? The Bitcoin community has proposed several solutions, each involving a trade-off between security, practicality, and the difficulty of modifying Bitcoin's rules.

Post-Quantum Signature Schemes

Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)

Cryptographic algorithms designed to withstand attacks from both classical and quantum computers.

Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)

Cryptographic algorithms designed to withstand attacks from both classical and quantum computers.

... Unlike existing algorithms (such as RSA and ECDSA), these algorithms are based on mathematical problems that quantum computers cannot solve efficiently. In August 2024, the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) finalized its first PQC standard. Several PQC signature schemes could potentially replace ECDSA in Bitcoin: BIP-360: QuBit Soft Fork Significant changes (such as adding a new signature scheme) are called "soft forks." They tighten the rules, allowing older software to continue functioning while providing new functionality. This is a backward-compatible change to Bitcoin's rules. Older nodes will still accept all new blocks as valid; they simply won't understand the new features. This is less disruptive than a "hard fork" (which forces everyone to upgrade or split the network). BIP-360, proposed by Hunter Beast in mid-2024, is currently the most concrete proposal to add quantum resistance to Bitcoin. It introduces:

  • A new output type called P2QRH (Pay-to-Quantum-Resistant-Hash) as part of SegWit version 3

  • Algorithmic flexibility, a single version byte allows the network to support multiple PQC algorithms, so even if one algorithm fails, others can be used

  • Designed for soft forking for backward compatibility

Commit - Reveal Scheme

Therefore, QSB uses GPUs (powerful graphics cards) to search trillions of candidate results until a combination is found that allows Bitcoin's own verification system to accept the hash output as a valid signature. Security no longer relies on "key" mathematics that quantum computers can crack. Instead, it relies on the irreversibility of hash operations, which quantum computers cannot overcome.

How it works: Three stages

Spending a QSB transaction involves three steps.

Each step involves massive computation, essentially trying billions of combinations until the right one is found, much like a combination lock with trillions of possibilities: Pinning: First, the system searches for specific combinations of transaction parameters to lock the payment to a specific destination. Think of it as a tightly sealed envelope, one that even a quantum attacker cannot change the recipient's name without breaking the seal. This requires trying approximately 70 trillion combinations. Proving Ownership (Digest Round, x2): The transaction script contains 150 pre-submitted keys (hashable and therefore hidden). To prove ownership, the payer needs to reveal a specific subset of these keys, chosen by solving another hash puzzle. This is the true quantum-safe "signature": an attacker needs to guess the correct subset from trillions of possibilities, where a quantum computer has no shortcuts. For added security, this step is run twice. Integrating All Information (Assembly): Once the GPU cluster finds a valid solution to all three puzzles, the owner's secure device (such as a hardware wallet) assembles the final transaction and broadcasts it to the network. Importantly, the heavy GPU computation only involves public data, much like hiring workers to search for specific grains of sand on a beach. The real secret (your "key") never leaves your secure device. Even if the GPU cluster is hacked, the attacker cannot obtain any useful information. Security and Cost: The most important point about QSB security is that Shor's algorithm (the quantum attack that threatens Bitcoin) offers no advantage over it. The entire scheme is built on hash functions, which quantum computers cannot effectively crack. Even the Grover algorithm (another quantum technology that can partially speed up hash operations) is far more secure than any practically attackable level. Regarding cost: the GPU computation required to complete a QSB transaction costs approximately $75 to $150 (recommended configuration, renting cloud GPUs). A lighter configuration costs approximately $40 to $80. Computation can be distributed across any number of GPUs; the more GPUs, the faster the speed, similar to how Bitcoin mining works. Limitations QSB is only applicable to older Bitcoin transactions. It relies on an older feature called FindAndDelete, which was removed in Bitcoin's 2017 SegWit upgrade. It cannot secure Bitcoins with modern address formats (SegWit, Taproot), supporting only the older P2SH format. It cannot be sent over regular networks. Regular Bitcoin nodes will reject QSB transactions as non-standard transactions. They must be submitted directly to mining pools willing to accept them. It is costly and complex. Each transaction requires a GPU cost of $75 to $150. Additionally, you need to manage special keys on secure devices and coordinate with miners. This is far from a simple "scan and send" approach. Breaking Bitcoin's limitations. The recommended setup uses exactly 201 opcodes (the absolute maximum allowed by Bitcoin) and nearly reaches the 10,000-byte script limit. There's no room to add other features. It also doesn't support the Lightning Network (Bitcoin's Faster Payments layer). Lost tokens cannot be recovered. Like all other proposals, QSB requires users to actively transfer tokens. If you lose your keys (such as Satoshi Nakamoto's tokens), they remain vulnerable. Early stages. As of April 2026, the GPU search engine has been implemented and demonstrated, but end-to-end transaction assembly and on-chain broadcasting are not yet complete. Part 8: Solution Comparison After examining all these proposals, I'd like to compare them side-by-side. Here is the comparison table I've compiled: Part 9: Timeline and Conclusion After completing all this research, here is my understanding of the timeline. It's not as clear as I expected when I started writing this article.

Lessons I Learned from All This

The threat is real and closer than previously thought. A paper published by Google in March 2026 reduced the number of physical qubits required by a factor of 20, to below 500,000. Current hardware capacity is about 500 times less than expected, instead of the previously thought 10,000 times. Google estimates a 10% probability of a "Quantum Day" by 2032.

SHA-256 mining is secure. Grover's algorithm does not pose a real threat to Bitcoin's proof-of-work. The real danger lies in the signing process, not in the mining itself.

SHA-256 mining is secure. Grover's algorithm does not pose a real threat to Bitcoin's proof-of-work. The real danger lies in the signing process, not in the mining itself.

Approximately 6.9 million bitcoins (about 35% of the total supply) are at risk because their public keys have been compromised. This includes about 1.7 million bitcoins output by Satoshi Nakamoto via P2PK, and the ever-growing number of bitcoins in Taproot (P2TR) outputs. A Google paper states that Taproot (P2TR) has the same vulnerability level as P2PK. Taproot is a quantum-safe step backward. Similar to P2PK, P2TR exposes public keys on-chain and will account for 21.68% of all bitcoin transactions by 2025. On a fast-clocked CRQC, a 9-minute attack is feasible, meaning there is approximately a 41% probability that the private key can be derived within a single bitcoin block interval. This means that in-spending attacks against all transaction types will become a reality. BIP-360 is the most comprehensive long-term solution, but it requires community consensus and years of deployment. QSB is a remarkable technical achievement; it's a quantum-safe scheme operating under Bitcoin's existing rules. Due to its cost, complexity, and scripting limitations, it can only serve as an emergency escape plan, not a general-purpose solution. Now is the time to act. Google's paper explicitly urges all vulnerable cryptocurrency communities to "immediately join the migration to PQC." Bitcoin governance is slow; Taproot took approximately four years from proposal to activation. Quantum-resistant upgrades may take even longer, and the window of opportunity may be shorter than anticipated. When I started this research, I initially thought I would conclude, "It's okay, we still have decades." But I can't say that now. As a Bitcoin enthusiast, I believe the community needs to take this issue seriously, not with panic, but with the same clear-headed and pragmatic approach that Bitcoin has taken to address all challenges to date. If this article has helped you better understand the quantum threat, please share it. The more Bitcoin enthusiasts truly understand this issue, the greater our chances of solving it together.

data.title: SEC's repeal of intraday trading restrictions: a retail investor frenzy or a risk trap?
data.description: Recently, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) formally approved amendments to Section 15c3-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, repealing the "Pattern Day Trader" (PTT) rule that had been in effect for over two decades. The core of this reform is the elimination of the hard threshold requiring account assets to remain above $25,000 for day trading, replacing it with a more complex set of "risk-based margin requirements." This decision is not an isolated event, but rather an inevitable result of the regulatory framework adapting to profound changes in market structure. From the meme stock frenzy of 2020, a "retail investor vs. Wall Street" battle, to the widespread adoption of zero-commission brokerages, and the rise of AI-driven quantitative strategies, retail investors have transformed from peripheral market observers into indispensable liquidity providers and price discovery participants. The old rules, designed to protect small investors perceived as lacking experience and capital from the rapid losses associated with high-frequency trading, have now shifted their balance between protection and market efficiency due to technological advancements and increased participant sophistication. The core of the rule change: from "capital threshold" to "risk management." This rule revision is far more than a simple "deregulation"; it represents a fundamental shift in regulatory logic. The core changes are reflected in three aspects. First, and most notably, is the elimination of the $25,000 minimum asset threshold. Previously, any investor who executed four or more intraday trades within five trading days, and whose account equity was below $25,000, would have their account labeled a PDT (Private Day Trader) and restricted from opening new positions for 90 days, or until sufficient funds were deposited. This threshold was seen by many critics as a "class barrier" excluding small and medium-sized investors from specific trading strategies. The new regulations remove this barrier, theoretically allowing accounts of any size to participate in intraday trading on an equal footing. Secondly, the regulatory label of "intraday trader" has been officially abolished. This means that brokerages no longer need to forcibly classify clients and impose special restrictions based on trading frequency. The focus of regulation has shifted from identifying and restricting "specific types" of traders to monitoring and managing the risks associated with "specific types" of trading behavior. Thirdly, and most significantly, is the introduction of a "risk-based margin framework." The new regulations require brokerages to implement a unified, real-time risk-based margin calculation and management system for all securities transactions of their clients (including cash accounts and margin accounts). Brokerages are no longer simply obligated to count "four trades," but must establish and maintain a system capable of dynamically assessing the concentration risk, liquidity risk, and market risk of client holdings, and calculating and collecting corresponding margin accordingly. In short, the regulatory logic has shifted from "limiting the number of trades you can make" to "ensuring you have sufficient capital to cover the risks associated with your trades." This essentially applies an institutional-level risk management framework, partially to the retail trading sector. The driving force behind this change: a paradigm shift in market structure. This move by the SEC is not a spur-of-the-moment decision, but a formal response to the dramatic changes in the market ecosystem over the past five years. The convergence of multiple forces has made the old rules increasingly outdated. The rise and qualitative change of retail investor power. The GameStop incident in early 2021 was a watershed moment, dramatically demonstrating the market influence that retail investors organized through social media could wield. But the deeper change lies in the fact that retail investors are no longer simply information recipients and trend followers. They share information and collaborate on strategies through platforms like Reddit and Discord, utilizing tools provided by zero-commission brokers for complex options trading strategies. According to public data, the proportion of retail trading orders in the total trading volume of the US stock market has jumped from about 10% in 2019 to over 20% in recent years, and even higher for some highly volatile stocks and options contracts. Retail investors have become one of the core pillars of market liquidity, and the old rules that use "protection" as a pretext for "restriction" are no longer sustainable. The revolutionary advancements in trading technology are also a factor. When the PDT (Priority Trading Targeting) rules were established twenty years ago, real-time risk monitoring technology was not yet mature, making it difficult for brokers to dynamically assess the cascading risks that high-frequency trading in small accounts might trigger. Today, cloud computing, big data, and artificial intelligence technologies have made real-time, accurate risk assessment possible. Mainstream brokerage platforms can calculate the Greeks risk of client positions, potential losses under stress tests, and margin requirements within milliseconds. Technology has made "prevention" a more effective risk control method than "punishment," providing the material basis for the shift in regulations towards a "risk-driven" approach. The evolution of brokerage business models and their profit demands: Zero commissions have become the industry standard, and brokerage revenues heavily rely on order flow payments, margin trading interest, and value-added services. Increased trading frequency directly impacts the first two revenue streams. Abolishing PDT (Private Trading Transaction) restrictions could theoretically stimulate trading activity and bring greater profits to brokerages. Therefore, zero-commission brokerages, represented by Robinhood, have been a significant lobbying force for this reform. Behind this lies the inevitable requirement for brokerages to transform from simple transaction execution channels into comprehensive financial service platforms driven by fintech. A Multi-Dimensional Perspective on Market Impact: Opportunities and Challenges Coexist. The impact of the new regulations will unfold in a multi-layered and gradual manner, and its effects far exceed what can be summarized as simply "benefiting retail investors." In the short term: a reshaping of liquidity structure and volatility characteristics. It is foreseeable that some active traders previously constrained by capital thresholds will increase their intraday trading frequency, especially in micro-contracts, low-priced stocks, and highly volatile technology and cryptocurrency stocks. This may bring additional liquidity to these assets, but it may also amplify their intraday volatility. The market may need to adapt to a short-term price formation mechanism driven by more "day trading." For the options market, trading in near-month at-the-money or slightly out-of-the-money options may be more active, as they are commonly used leverage tools by intraday traders. In the medium term: accelerated differentiation among retail investors. Relaxing the rules on trading frequency is equivalent to removing a "mandatory cooling-off period." It completely relinquishes the autonomy of trading decisions and the responsibility for risk-taking to individual investors. The inevitable result is an exacerbation of polarization among retail investors. Traders with strict discipline, effective strategies, and risk management capabilities may utilize more flexible tools to improve capital efficiency; while investors who rely more on intuition and chase highs and lows may deplete their capital more quickly through frequent trading. Historical data shows that in an unrestricted environment, the overall net return of retail investors is often negative, and high-frequency trading amplifies this trend. The new regulations may not change the long-term distribution of "seven losses, two break-evens, and one profit," but they will accelerate this process. In the long term: the "quasi-institutionalization" of retail trading and the increasing complexity of regulation. The most ironic and profound impact is that this margin system, centered on "risk," is subtly educating and requiring retail investors to think and manage trades in a way closer to that of institutions. Focusing on risk exposure, margin utilization, and stress testing scenarios—these are the daily tasks of fund managers, and may become essential skills for active retail investors in the future. Market participants' behavioral patterns will tend to become more homogenized. At the same time, the regulatory challenge shifts from "setting simple rules" to "assessing the effectiveness of complex risk control systems." The SEC needs to ensure that the risk models of various brokerages are prudent and consistent, preventing them from relaxing standards under competitive pressure. This will test the regulator's technical capabilities and resources. From an investment perspective, rule changes will reshape the business value and investment logic of related fields. Direct beneficiaries: Brokerages and trading technology providers. The potential increase in trading activity will most directly benefit brokerages with a high proportion of retail brokerage business, especially those platforms with a large user base and active client trading. In addition, fintech companies that provide brokerages with real-time risk calculation engines and compliance monitoring systems are expected to see increased demand for their products. Investors should pay attention to the trends in these companies' quarterly financial reports regarding average daily revenue per user, order flow revenue, and margin interest income. Strategic Opportunities: High Volatility and High Liquidity Sectors. Rule changes may alter the battleground for different investment styles. Two asset classes warrant attention: first, assets inherently possessing high volatility and topicality, such as stocks in cutting-edge fields like artificial intelligence and biotechnology, which are more likely to attract intraday trading funds; second, ETFs and large-cap tech stock options with good liquidity and narrow spreads, providing a solid foundation for intraday strategies. The weight of trading behavior itself may increase among short-term price drivers. Risk Warning: Micro-level Accumulation of Systemic Risk. While the new regulations aim to manage risk through more refined risk control, it cannot be ignored that when a large number of retail investors adopt convergent trading behaviors under similar risk models (e.g., being simultaneously required to add margin or facing forced liquidation during periods of soaring volatility), it could trigger unexpected liquidity shortages and a "stampede" effect. The volatility-induced margin call events of February 2018 and the short squeeze in 2021 both revealed new pathways for external volatility to propagate to the center in modern markets. Regulators and market participants alike need to be vigilant against these new signs of systemic risk triggered by the collective behavior of retail investors. Conclusion: A Rebalancing of Freedom and Responsibility The SEC's repeal of the PDT rule marks the end of an era. It acknowledges the increased status and capabilities of retail investors in the market and reflects a shift in regulatory philosophy from paternalistic protection to adult self-responsibility. Essentially, this is a large-scale social experiment: entrusting more complex tools and a freer environment to a group with vastly different levels of education and risk tolerance. For individual investors, the real challenge lies not in whether they can conduct a fourth or fifth intraday trade, but in whether they possess the corresponding risk awareness, strategy system, and emotional control. Rules are merely the stage; profitability still stems from superior understanding and execution compared to the market average. This transformation may give rise to a new generation of retail trading masters, but more likely, it will make the redistribution of market wealth more efficient and faster. While celebrating the arrival of trading freedom, every market participant should re-examine their investment philosophy: Are you investing in the future of businesses, or trading on the emotions of others? Does your advantage come from information, technology, discipline, or simply luck? The market's answer is always more brutal and more realistic than regulatory rules.

data.title: Musk's X doesn't want to just earn advertising revenue anymore.
data.description: On April 14, 2026, Platform X shoved a market data terminal into your timeline. You see a tweet mentioning that $BONK rose 40%, and you click on the code. Instead of redirecting to TradingView or opening a brokerage app—the market data chart appears directly in the post. Real-time prices, 24-hour percentage changes, market capitalization, and interactive charts from daily to yearly data are all displayed within the app, without redirection. After viewing the chart, there's a button next to it: Trade. This is more aggressive than the Bloomberg terminal. Bloomberg requires you to actively seek it out; X shoves it in your face while you're scrolling through social media. X has approximately 600 million monthly active users. How many of these people would click the buy button at 2 AM after seeing a viral tweet? That's exactly the question X is testing. The person driving this is Nikita Bier. His business card identifies him as the product manager at X, but he was previously an advisor to the Solana Foundation. This isn't a small detail—he joined X not to optimize the tweet experience, but to provide a liquidity pipeline for crypto assets within social media. Joining him at the same time is Benji Taylor, the former head of design for the Coinbase Base network. The combined backgrounds of these two clearly illustrate what X wants to achieve: not just a market data display window, but a complete closed loop from content discovery to fund execution. A social platform is redefining itself as a financial gateway. From tweets to orders, X has built three layers. Looking from the outside in, what X is building is divided into three layers. The outermost layer is Smart Cashtags. For over a decade, #BTC on X was just a search link, leading to a bunch of tweets. After April 14, 2026, this link will become an embedded market data card—real-time price, volume, market capitalization, plus interactive charts with adjustable time spans from daily to yearly, all presented within the app. What's even more interesting is the asset coverage. It not only supports mainstream US stocks and leading crypto assets, but also allows direct input of smart contract addresses. In other words, even newly issued tokens that haven't yet been listed on any centralized exchange can have their market data displayed on X as long as you have a contract address. This feature directly transforms X into a public showcase for on-chain long-tail assets. To reduce confusion from altcoins, X also introduces a predictive suggestion algorithm: when a user enters a code, the system automatically recommends matching assets. The middle layer is Wealthsimple's one-click trading pilot. Canadian users see an additional "Trade" button on their cards when viewing market data. Using deep linking technology, clicking directly leads to Wealthsimple's order placement interface. The entire path is: Discover tweet → View market data → Click buy, without leaving X. Wealthsimple is Canada's largest online broker, managing over CAD 100 billion in assets and serving over 3 million users. X maintains clear legal boundaries here: X does not act as a broker, does not directly execute trades, and does not handle user funds. All regulated trading activities are handled by Wealthsimple. This allowed X to bypass the lengthy compliance approval process with multinational brokerages, quickly entering the market as an "interface provider." To promote this feature, Wealthsimple offered a CAD 42 reward for new users—earned upon a minimum deposit of CAD 1 and holding for 180 days. The number "42" is a Musk-esque Easter egg: the answer to the ultimate question about the universe from *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy*. At the innermost layer, and the least discussed, is X Money. It offers a 6% annualized return savings account, managed by Cross River Bank, with FDIC insurance up to $250,000; a metallic black Visa debit card bearing the user's X account name; and instant P2P transfers via Visa Direct, supporting direct salary deposits. X Payments LLC holds money transfer licenses in all 40 states and DCs in the US. From displaying market data to executing trades to managing funds, X has integrated the entire process into a single app. On the surface, this seems like a product update capitalizing on the crypto hype. But if you look closely at what Musk and his team are doing, you'll find that this isn't just riding the wave; it's a fundamental shift. Advertising has been X's revenue pillar in the past, but it's also its Achilles' heel. Advertising follows economic cycles, advertiser sentiments, and platform reputation. After Musk took over X, many advertisers withdrew, and the platform's revenue experienced a significant decline. Financial services are different. A user makes a transaction on the platform, and the platform generates fees. Users deposit their salaries into X Money, and the interest rate spread increases. Users open the app daily to check market conditions and buy/sell assets, creating user stickiness. This logic doesn't rely on advertisers' goodwill, but only on users' financial activities. Polymarket partner Tat Thang put it more directly: Cryptocurrency transaction fees may become X's most sustainable source of revenue. Compared to advertising, it's more stable and scalable. Musk's own ambitions are even greater. His original words were: "I hope X will become 'the platform where all the money is.'" This isn't a metaphor; it's a product roadmap. He wants a super platform that integrates P2P payments, high-yield savings, stock trading, crypto trading, and prediction contracts—a WeChat-like platform, but more aggressive. What happened after WeChat integrated payments through WeChat Pay? Users didn't leave, data remained, consumption behavior was recorded, and commercialization expanded infinitely. X's path goes even further. Bill has Solana DNA, Taylor has Coinbase background, and X's native support for on-chain long-tail assets is significantly higher than any traditional financial platform. Bill publicly stated that X's goal is to become "the preferred destination for the crypto community." This platform used to make money from attention. Now it wants to make money from attention and then take that money directly. But who dares to put their money in? But buying stocks on X is a bit strange. Before launching Smart Cashtags, X did one thing: a massive cleanup of cryptocurrency bot accounts on the platform. Tat Thang calls this a “mandatory compliance cleanup”—before deploying consumer finance products, the platform must be free of phishing links and fake trading signals. Without this, any in-site trading functionality faces significant legal risks. The problem is, the cleanup of bots is endless. The quality of X's content remains inconsistent. In the Reddit discussion about the $42 reward, many voices expressed: “I don’t have confidence in X’s privacy policy and don’t want to put money in it.” Others directly stated that Musk’s personal political stance has made them consider deleting their accounts, let alone depositing money. Another troubling issue is the prediction contract license obtained by Wealthsimple. Canadian regulator CIRO has approved Wealthsimple to offer predictive contracts based on real-world events—contracts related to economic indicators, financial markets, and climate trends. CIRO defines these as compliant financial derivatives, but several experts warn that such products "walk the line between investment and gambling." Placing highly leveraged, binary-advantageous products within a social media trading platform means that decisions made by users in a state of heightened emotion have unpredictable consequences. In the competitive landscape, Robinhood launched "Robinhood Social" in March 2026, allowing users to discuss market trends, share positions, and track the investment activities of members of parliament within its trading app. Both companies are vying for the same user base, but their paths are reversed: X started with social networking and expanded into finance, while Robinhood started with trading and expanded into social networking; ultimately, they are competing for the same wallet. Compared to the collaboration with eToro three years ago, which involved brand sponsorship and external links, users still had to leave X to trade. This time, Wealthsimple's "Trade" button is directly embedded in the emoticon card, eliminating the need for users to leave. This shift from collaboration to deep integration represents a qualitative change. X doesn't intend to be a traffic distribution platform; it wants to control the transaction entry point itself. Summary: X will either become the next WeChat, or it will become a place everyone pays attention to, but no one actually invests in. That buy button is already on your timeline.

data.title: What positions will the next Federal Reserve Chairman hold?
data.description:
Kevin Warsh, a leading candidate for the next Federal Reserve Chairman, recently submitted a 69-page financial disclosure document for his Senate hearing. It not only disclosed Warsh's vast assets, amounting to hundreds of millions of dollars, but also revealed his large and extremely cutting-edge crypto-related holdings. The investment portfolio not only covers mainstream public chains and DeFi projects, but also includes the highly experimental algorithmic stablecoin of 2018, Basis. His depth and forward-thinking involvement in the crypto space far surpasses that of any traditional Wall Street elite or Washington policymaker, and even surpasses many crypto novices.

Basis And algorithmic central bank

In the asset list on the  69  page, lang="EN-US">

Top 5%Risk Preference and Venture Capital Strategy

Judging from the disclosed holdings list,Kevin Warsh's investment path differs from conventional secondary market transactions.Kevin Warsh's investment path differs from conventional secondary market transactions.Kevin Warsh's investment path differs from conventional secondary market transactions. Crypto assets are often held indirectly through limited partners (LPs) of leading industry funds such as Polychain, which is a typical primary market venture capital strategy. This configuration exhibits a clear combination of characteristics: "Beta + Alpha". In terms of underlying assets, he heavily invested in high-performance public chains such as Solana and Ethereum networks such as Optimism. These types of assets are generally seen as capturing the growth in blockchain network usage. text=""> Beta Basics. At the application layer, funds flow to dYdX (decentralized derivatives), Compound (on-chain lending), and Lighter (order book). lang="EN-US"> DEX) and other infrastructures. The portfolio also includes projects with more complex mechanisms and higher risk factors: early-stage algorithmic stablecoins and projects emphasizing native yields (…). Native Yield and the points-based distribution mechanism of Layer 2 networks. These two names would seem out of place on the asset list of any traditional Wall Street professional. The portfolio also includes Polymarket, prediction markets, and decentralized social networks, projects that have long operated in a regulatory gray area: Polymarket was once... The CFTC imposed heavy penalties, and the industry continues to be scrutinized by regulators during election betting cycles; DeSo, on the other hand, attempts to shake the information distribution logic monopolized by giants from the ground up. These holdings share a common underlying understanding: Warsh views crypto projects as software protocols that optimize existing clearing and settlement systems, rather than as bargaining chips for asset speculation. This configuration logic, based on infrastructure and financial efficiency, gave him a practical understanding of the operating mechanism of decentralized networks that went far beyond the conceptual level. Beyond those encryption protocols, what firmly holds sway are the family-owned shares of established consumer giants like Estée Lauder, board seats at UPS and Coupang, and single transactions exceeding $50 million. Juggernaut Fund is a large-scale private equity macro fund. Its capital reach extends to cutting-edge fields such as SpaceX and Delphi AI. This configuration structure, spanning traditional macroeconomics, core infrastructure, and on-chain protocols, breaks away from the one-sided interpretation of cryptocurrency hype. It's closer to the underlying logic of top investors: using stable assets as a foundation and betting on the future with high-yield assets. The Dual-Track Logic of Regulation and the Liquidation Paradox The attitude towards the crypto world, Warsh, exhibits a very real double standard. He demonstrated the inclusiveness of traditional capital when facing Bitcoin, openly acknowledging that BTC is a high-quality asset for hedging against excessive fiat currency issuance. However, this inclusiveness completely disappears when faced with DeFi and privately-owned stablecoins that attempt to restructure credit or even bypass central banks to issue their own currencies. In the past, traditional Federal Reserve officials, lacking technical expertise, could only manage the market crudely through interest rate hikes and balance sheet reduction. But Warsh is different. He personally ran smart contracts, calculated protocol yields, and clearly understands where the Achilles' heel of these decentralized projects lies. This means that his future regulation will not be a haphazard, indiscriminate approach by laymen, but rather a precise, targeted crackdown. He has only one criterion for judging everything: whether this innovation threatens the sovereign status of the dollar. According to Washington's compliance requirements, once the hearing is passed, Warsh must completely liquidate all crypto assets before officially taking office. While the tokens on his account will be wiped to zero, his years of experience in the primary market will propel him to the absolute center of global financial power. In a sense, this prospective Federal Reserve Chairman's significant purchase was a high-level endorsement of the underlying technological value of Web3. The old bureaucrats who only understand traditional fiat currency models are exiting the stage. With the exposure of this asset list, the crypto industry is about to face not blind suppression, but a complete reshuffling led by truly knowledgeable top competitors.

Kevin WarshFinancial Disclosure: Core Holdings Overview (Crypto++Important Non-Blockchain Projects)

Of whichJuggernaut Fund LPis the largest holding, with each of the two positions exceeding5000$ (totaling over1billion US dollars), part of THSDFS LLCa single position of 100Ten thousand-500Ten thousand US dollars.

In addition, he also passed Abstract Holdings(DCM series), AVF/AVGFSeries,THSDFS LLCIndirectly held by many funds such as Coworker AI, Flashnet, Kinetic, Mirror、Bessemerand dozens of other projects. The combined assets of him and his wife are approximately US$131 million to US$209 million, or nearly US$69 million, according to nearly 69 pages of disclosure documents. Of the 1800 assets, the vast majority are non-blockchain traditional finance and cutting-edge technology. While crypto holdings number only around 30, they represent a significant portion of deep VC (Capital Markets) investment.

data.title: Kalshi Targets Underage Access Problem With New Parent Verification System And AI Tools For Identity Confirmation
data.description:
Minors bypassing age verification on prediction markets has prompted Kalshi to tighten its controls, with the company rolling out new tools aimed at stopping underage access and strengthening identity checks across its platform.

Parent Portal Targets ID Misuse Among Families

Kalshi co-founder and chief executive Tarek Mansour said the firm is preparing to launch a “portal for parents” designed to help detect when a child may be using a parent’s identity to access the platform.

🚨 Kalshi to Launch Parent Portal for Prediction Market Monitoring

The prediction market platform will introduce a portal allowing parents to monitor and restrict their children's accounts, according to CEO Tarek Mansour. The move aims to prevent minors from using parental…

— Crynet (@crynetio) April 15, 2026

Speaking at the Semafor World Economy conference in Washington, DC, Mansour explained the idea behind the system:

“We are launching a portal for parents to basically submit their identification, even if they don’t want to be users of Kalshi, to see if someone is using it. Because then they can see if their children are using their ID and police it.”

The system is designed to tackle cases where minors bypass the US 18+ requirement by registering with a parent’s personal details, including sensitive identity information.

Kalshi CEO Tarek Mansour says the platform is ‘launching a portal for parents’.

“We are launching a portal for parents to basically submit their identification, even if they don’t want to be users of Kalshi, to see if someone is using it. Because then they can see if their…

— Semafor (@semafor) April 15, 2026

Selfie Verification And AI Checks To Flag Fake Identities

Alongside the parent portal, Kalshi is introducing selfie-based verification during account setup, with artificial intelligence used to compare facial images against submitted ID documents.

Mansour said,

“We are also adding selfies to accounts, where you can basically look at the face of a person, and it can tell you obviously if this person is not the actual parent that’s 50 years old.”

He added that further safeguards are still being developed.

The aim is to reduce cases where accounts are opened using borrowed or stolen identity documents and ensure the person trading on the platform matches the verified user.

He added that there would also be AI tools to see if a Kalshi user’s selfie looks like the person whose ID was used to set up the account.

“We are also adding selfies to accounts, where you can basically look at the face of a person, and it can tell you obviously if this person…

— Semafor (@semafor) April 15, 2026

Family Accounts Bring Monitoring Features To Trading Activity

Kalshi is also exploring “family accounts”, allowing users to track activity across connected profiles, with a focus on encouraging accountability within households and social circles.

Mansour said,

“How do we kind of create a sort of accountability structure amongst friends and families for people to be like, ‘Hey, you might be doing a little too much here.’ We want this to be a tool for good, not a tool for excessive behaviors.”

The feature would allow shared oversight of trading behaviour, adding another layer of monitoring beyond standard account controls.

Regulatory Pressure Builds As States Challenge Prediction Markets

The move comes as prediction markets face increasing scrutiny across the United States, particularly over sports-related contracts and gambling classifications.

Kalshi recently faced a $5 million fine in Ohio, where regulators raised concerns that users under 21 could access the platform despite state-level gambling restrictions. Federal rules for prediction markets set the minimum age at 18.

Ohio put Kalshi on notice today that its “prediction markets” are unlawful gaming and proposed a $5 million fine. A federal court already agreed with our reading of the law.

I wouldn’t bet on how long Kalshi will be operating in Ohio. pic.twitter.com/Gcw3GOTTlk

— Dave Yost (@DaveYostOH) April 14, 2026

At the centre of Kalshi’s legal position is its argument that it falls under the exclusive oversight of the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), rather than state gambling regulators.

The agency has fewer than 600 staff, according to government records, and has taken a limited enforcement approach in some areas of compliance, focusing on willful breaches rather than technical violations.

Court Battles And Rival Platforms Add Pressure On Kalshi

Legal disputes continue across several states, with courts split over how prediction markets should be regulated.

A federal judge in Arizona recently blocked state authorities from enforcing gambling laws against Kalshi’s event contracts, following a similar ruling in New Jersey where a federal appellate court found that federal commodities law may override state gambling rules.

🚨BREAKING: Arizona federal court GRANTS the CFTC's motion to enjoin Arizona's criminal prosecution of Kalshi. Monday's arraignment will not go forward. https://t.co/l5WgpAQguD

— Stefan Schropp (@SPSchropp) April 10, 2026

At the same time, competition in the sector is increasing.

Binance has recently introduced prediction market features within its wallet app, while Crypto.com has partnered with High Roller Technologies to expand similar offerings, adding further pressure on Kalshi’s position in the market.

data.title: Truth Terminal: 10 Polymarkets for Understanding Crypto Sentiment
data.description:
Author: Stacy Muur Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Everyone has an opinion on the direction of the crypto market. Most opinions are worthless, and indeed, that's all they're worth.

Polymarket is completely different.

You can't just express an opinion: you have to bet. Real money, real odds, and real consequences if you're wrong. When a question (like "How much will Bitcoin go in 2026?") sees over $30 million flowing in, the market's collective answer starts to carry weight.

This isn't because market predictions are always right. It's because people can brag for free on Twitter, but they almost never lie about their money.

That's the value of Polymarket as a sentiment indicator: not in accuracy, but in honesty.

The real issue lies in the gap between the heated discussions on Twitter and the actual pricing in the market. I scoured Polymarket's crypto market and selected 10 assets that best reflect the current true sentiment of capital: covering Bitcoin, altcoins, stablecoins, and macroeconomic risks. Some have deep liquidity, some have extremely thin market capitalization, but both convey information. 1. What price level will Bitcoin reach in 2026?

Trading Volume: $30.7 million | End Date: January 1, 2027 | Liquidity: High

Core Odds:

  • Breakout $75,000: 100%

  • Breakout $90,000: 100%

  • Breakout $100,000: 35%

  • Breakout $110,000: 24%

  • Breakout $150,000: 10%

  • Breakout $250,000: 4% $55,000: 68% $35,000: 25% A move to $100,000 is at best a 50/50 chance. Anything above $150,000 is largely priced in as noise: the "$200,000 by the end of the year" claims on Twitter lack real-world support. However, most people overlook one point: the market believes there's a 68% probability that Bitcoin will fall to $55,000 at some point. The baseline scenario isn't "only rising, never falling," but rather a volatile, upward trend. The range is large, and optimism should be restrained, not frenzied.

    2. When will Bitcoin reach a new all-time high?

    Trading Volume: $6.07 million | End Date: July 1, 2026 | Liquidity: Medium

    Core Odds:

    • Before June 30, 2026: 3%

    • Before September 30, 2026: 10%

    • Before December 31, 2026: 15%

    The probability of Bitcoin reaching a new high before the end of the year is only 15%. For a market that claims to have just started a new cycle, confidence is surprisingly low.

The short-term odds are even more telling: only 3% before June. The market believes there's no catalyst for a breakout in the short term. This directly contradicts the mainstream narrative on Twitter that "the bull market has just begun." 6 million trades mean this isn't a minority opinion: funds believe Bitcoin's new high will either be later than 2026, or it won't happen at all in that time. 3. Will Bitcoin reach $80,000 or $100,000 first?

Trading Volume: $328,000 | Status: Settled | Result: Reaching $80,000 First (Probability > 99%)

This market has settled, and the answer is obvious. The question is simple: will Bitcoin fall below $80,000 first, or surge above $100,000 first? The result is vastly different, almost a certainty that it will reach $80,000 first.

Even if the market as a whole is optimistic about Bitcoin's upward trend in 2026, funds unanimously believe that it will fall first and then rise. If you bet on it breaking $100,000 first, you are on the opposite side of the > 99% consensus. This is also consistent with the first market: a 68% probability of falling to $55,000. Upward movement is never a straight line.

4. Will the total market capitalization of stablecoins exceed $500 billion before 2027?

4. Will the total market capitalization of stablecoins exceed $500 billion before 2027?

Trading Volume: $569,000 | End Date: January 1, 2027 | Liquidity: Low

Core Odds:

  • Yes: 17%

  • No: 83%

The current market capitalization of stablecoins is approximately $300 billion. Reaching $500 billion by the end of the year means nearly doubling in less than 9 months.

Everyone's talking about the widespread adoption of stablecoins, as if it were an inevitable trend—Fidelity launched its stablecoin, Western Union joined the competition, and Polymarket is developing its own. And this growth is indeed happening. But market acceptance of stablecoins, which peaked at 45% in January, has since plummeted to 17%. People are closely watching the actual pace of progress and adjusting their strategies accordingly. From $300 billion to $500 billion, it's not just about increasing cryptocurrency adoption.
